sources.notes: Statement and constraints: count possible complementary-sum sets Q of size n with all elements in [1,x].
Official editorial/code confirms canonicalizing by distinct values and counting excess with an unbounded knapsack-style DP.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate checked by search/open attempt; not used as a consulted solution source.

annotation.summary: Count distinct sets Q of n complementary sums that can come from a positive integer multiset and have maximum value at most x.
annotation.constraints: The useful n range ends once the minimum possible maximum n(n+1)/2-1 exceeds x; remaining work is coefficient DP over the excess.
annotation.core_idea: A set Q determines a canonical multiset A: one value is 1 with some multiplicity, and the other n-1 values are distinct increasing values >=2. The smallest maximum complementary sum is n(n+1)/2-1. Extra budget above that minimum can be distributed among the multiplicity of 1 and the nondecreasing gaps of the distinct values, giving generating function 1/(1-z)^2 * product_{w=2}^{n-1} 1/(1-z^w). Compute coefficients by unbounded knapsack and sum all maxima <= x.
tricks: canonical representative with one repeated value 1
convert strict increasing values to gap variables

solution_variants.name: canonical-gap-knapsack
solution_variants.summary: After canonicalization, count ways to distribute the excess maximum value using two unit generators and generators 2..n-1.

tags.evidence: The answer is a coefficient/counting problem over canonical multisets and gap variables.
The coefficient extraction is implemented as a DP counting representations of each excess value.
The recurrence is an unbounded knapsack over allowed gap weights.
The key reduction is choosing a unique representative multiset with repeated 1s and distinct larger values.
tags.description: Reduce equivalent objects to a unique normalized representative before counting or optimizing.
tags.created_reason: This problem's decisive step is not just DP/combinatorics; the canonical representative removes multiset equivalence and makes the count tractable.
